title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
Note



Audience: These notes build on the Shiny Apps lecture. They explain Shinylive — a technology that runs Shiny apps entirely in the user’s browser, with no R server required. We compare it to traditional Shiny and explore when to use each approach.

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
1. What Is Shinylive?

Prompt
What is Shinylive? How is it different from a traditional Shiny app?


Response
Shinylive is a technology that allows Shiny apps to run entirely in the web browser — with no R or Python server required on the backend.
In a traditional Shiny app, when a user interacts with a slider or dropdown, their action is sent over the internet to an R process running on a server. The server recomputes and sends results back to the browser. The server is always in the loop.
Shinylive changes this completely. Using WebAssembly (Wasm), it compiles R (via webR) so that it runs inside the browser itself. There is no server. The entire R runtime — your app code, data, packages — lives in the user’s browser tab.









Traditional Shiny
Shinylive




Architecture
Browser → Internet → R Server → Browser
Browser only — R runs via WebAssembly


Server needed?
Yes
No

title: Essay on Shiny Live
section: 2. How Shinylive Works — Under the Hood
text:
2. How Shinylive Works — Under the Hood

Prompt
Walk me through what happens technically when a user opens a Shinylive app.


Response
Here is the sequence of events when a user navigates to a Shinylive-powered page:

The browser downloads the page — a static HTML/JS/CSS bundle (no server required).
WebR loads — the browser downloads the webR runtime (~30–60 MB on first load). This is the compiled R interpreter in WebAssembly format. It is cached for future visits.
Packages are fetched — any R packages your app uses are downloaded from a WebAssembly package repository and loaded into the browser’s virtual filesystem.
Your Shiny app code executes — the R interpreter inside the browser runs your app.R (or ui.R/server.R).
Reactivity works normally — all of Shiny’s reactive magic runs in-browser. No network round-trips for reactivity. The user’s interactions are instantaneous.

This means the app can work offline once it has been loaded, and it can be hosted on any static file server — GitHub Pages, Netlify, Quarto Pub, Amazon S3, etc.

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
3. Getting Started with Shinylive

Prompt
How do I actually use Shinylive? Show me the steps.


Response
The simplest way to embed a Shinylive app is inside a Quarto .qmd file. First, add the extension:
# Run in terminal
quarto add quarto-ext/shinylive
Then add this to your _quarto.yml:
filters:
  - shinylive
Then embed an app using a shinylive-r code block:
```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 400

library(shiny)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Hello Shinylive!"),
  sliderInput("n", "Sample size:", 10, 500, 100),
  plotOutput("hist")
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  output$hist <- renderPlot({
    hist(rnorm(input$n), col = "steelblue", main = "Normal Distribution")
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

title: Essay on Shiny Live
section: 4. Shinylive vs. Traditional Shiny: A Detailed Comparison
text:
4. Shinylive vs. Traditional Shiny: A Detailed Comparison

Prompt
What are the specific differences between Shinylive and traditional Shiny?


Response
The table below compares the two approaches across key dimensions:








Dimension
Traditional Shiny
Shinylive




Server needed?
Yes — R must run on a server
No — R runs in the browser


Hosting cost
Potentially significant (shinyapps.io, Posit Connect)
Free — any static host works


Scalability
Limited by server resources
Infinitely scalable


Initial load time
Fast (server already running)
Slower first load (~30–60 s)


Reactivity speed
Network round-trip delay
Instant after load


Package support
Any CRAN or GitHub package
Only Wasm-compiled packages


Data privacy
Data processed on server
Data never leaves the browser


File system access
Full server file system
Virtual in-browser only


Database connections
Full support
Not supported


Offline use
Requires active internet
Works offline after first load

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
5. Pros and Cons of Shinylive

Prompt
Summarize the advantages and disadvantages of Shinylive compared to traditional Shiny.


Response


Advantages of Shinylive

Zero server cost. Deploy to GitHub Pages or Netlify for free. No monthly fees for shinyapps.io or Posit Connect.
Unlimited scalability. Because R runs in each user’s browser, there is no central server to overwhelm. A thousand concurrent users is the same as one.
No server maintenance. No need to manage R versions, package updates, or server uptime. Your static files just sit there.
Privacy by design. Sensitive data (e.g., uploaded CSV files) is processed entirely in the user’s browser and never transmitted to a server.
Works offline. Once loaded, the app continues to function without internet access — useful in field settings or areas with poor connectivity.
Perfect for education. Embed live, editable Shiny apps directly into Quarto documents, textbooks, or course websites. Students can interact without installing R.



Disadvantages of Shinylive

Slow initial load. The WebAssembly R runtime is large (~30–60 MB). First-time users may wait 30 seconds or more.
Limited package ecosystem. Not all CRAN packages have been compiled to WebAssembly. Packages with compiled C/C++ dependencies may not be available.
No server-side operations. You cannot connect to a database, call a private API, send emails, or write files to a server.
Constrained compute. The browser’s JavaScript engine and WebAssembly runtime are slower than a native R process. Complex models or large datasets may be sluggish.
Browser memory limits. Large datasets must fit in the user’s browser memory, not server RAM.
Debugging is harder. Error messages appear in the browser console, not the familiar RStudio console.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text: Audience: These notes are written for colleagues who are comfortable with R but have not yet built interactive web applications. By the end of this lecture, you will understand what Shiny is, how it works, and how to build your first app — no web development experience needed.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
1. What Is Shiny?

Prompt
What is R Shiny, and why should a data scientist care about it?


Response
Shiny is an open-source R package developed by Posit (formerly RStudio) that allows you to build interactive web applications directly from R — with no HTML, CSS, or JavaScript required (though you can use them if you want to).

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
2. A Complete Working Example

Prompt
Show a complete, self-contained Shiny app that demonstrates key concepts.


Response
The following app allows users to explore the mtcars dataset interactively. It demonstrates inputs, reactive outputs, a plot, and a table — all in one cohesive example.
library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)

ui <- fluidPage(
  
  theme = bslib::bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"),
  
  titlePanel("🚗 mtcars Explorer"),
  
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput("x_var", "X-axis variable:",
                  choices = c("Horsepower" = "hp",
                              "Weight"     = "wt",
                              "Displacement" = "disp"),
                  selected = "hp"),
      
      selectInput("color_var", "Color by:",
                  choices = c("Cylinders" = "cyl",
                              "Transmission" = "am"),
                  selected = "cyl"),
      
      sliderInput("alpha", "Point transparency:",
                  min = 0.1, max = 1, value = 0.7, step = 0.1),
      
      hr(),
      p("Data: Motor Trend Car Road Tests, 1974.")
    ),
    
    mainPanel(
      tabsetPanel(
        tabPanel("Plot", plotOutput("scatter", height = "400px")),
        tabPanel("Table", DT::dataTableOutput("table"))
      )
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  
  # Reactive dataset (shared between plot and table)
  car_data <- reactive({
    mtcars |>
      mutate(cyl = factor(cyl), am = factor(am, labels = c("Auto","Manual")))
  })
  
  # Scatter plot
  output$scatter <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(car_data(), aes_string(x = input$x_var, y = "mpg",
                                   color = input$color_var)) +
      geom_point(size = 3, alpha = input$alpha) +
      geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
      labs(title = paste("MPG vs.", input$x_var),
           y = "Miles Per Gallon") +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
  })
  
  # Data table
  output$table <- DT::renderDataTable({
    car_data()
  }, options = list(pageLength = 10))
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
3. Deploying a Shiny App

Prompt
How do you share a Shiny app with others who don’t have R installed?


Response
Since Shiny apps require an R server to process the reactive logic, deployment means running that server somewhere accessible.


Deployment Options








Option
Best For
Cost




shinyapps.io
Quick sharing, small apps
Free tier available


Posit Connect
Enterprise, teams
Paid


Shiny Server
Self-hosted on Linux
Free (open source)


Heroku / Docker
Custom infrastructure
Varies


Shinylive
Serverless, no R backend needed
Free (see next essay)



Deploying to shinyapps.io is the simplest path:
# Install the rsconnect package
install.packages("rsconnect")

# Configure your account (one-time setup)
rsconnect::setAccountInfo(
  name   = "your-username",
  token  = "YOUR_TOKEN",
  secret = "YOUR_SECRET"
)

# Deploy
rsconnect::deployApp("path/to/your/app/")
